description: Intro video teaches the basics of abdominal xray. Abdominal Xray has limited utility. But, there are still important uses, and diagnoses that you need to make on them. We introduce some concepts in the video. Cases that include the most important things you need to know on abdominal plain film are included in this course!

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Gas in the wall of the urinary bladder, in keeping with emphysematous cystitis

Other findings:

  • No dilated bowel
  • No free gas
  • Staple line over low pelvis (zoom in)
  • Vascular calcifications
  • Aside: if you are wondering what these are, this is just calcification of the costal cartilage. This is a common finding and nearly always incidental, age related.

Subsequent CT confirmed emphysematous cystitis / gas in the bladder wall:

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This is a case demonstrating gas where it shouldn't be (in this case, emphysematous cystitis).

Emphysematous cystitis occurs in UTI with gas-forming organisms -- most commonly in diabetics, elderly, immunocompromised. Most common organisms are E coli and Klebsiella.

Gas can be seen in the lumen or in the wall. In the wall is classic - rim /curvilinear in appearance (like this case). Seeing gas in the lumen is much more common, usually due to instrumentation (i.e. foley placement), and so is more nonspecific.

Exclude other causes on CT (fistulae to bowel, vagina, etc).

On Xray, differentiate a rectum filled with stool from a bladder with gas. Bladder is midline, in this location just above the symphysis, shaped like bladder (rounded and more triangular inferiorly), and anterior. Rectum will be more tubular, mottled/speckled gas (stool) rather than curvilinear. Sometimes appearance on Xray can overlap -- get a CT if in doubt.

cases.clinicalHistory: Known C diff, systemically unwell, r/o megacolon

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Weighted-tip NG tube with tip in the stomach
  • Very dilated transverse colon (notice incomplete haustral folds, haustra / sacculations, anatomy -- all confirming its colon)
  • Notice thickened proximal colon.

In the clinical context, findings raise suspicion for toxic megacolon.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
In severe colitis, inflammation can extend into muscularis / myenteric plexus (enteric nerves), which can cause severe ileus. This leads to gas/fluid accumulation, dilatation, increasing intraluminal pressure, and risk of ischemia/perforation.

Importantly, dilated colon in colitis does not automatically mean 'toxic megacolon'. There must be significant colonic dilatation AND systemic toxicity (requires correlation with clinical status).

Imaging:

  • Colonic Dilatation (most often transverse colon, as anti-dependent, > 6 cm), without mechanical obstruction
  • Eventual loss of haustration, mucosal edema may be present
  • Serial Xrays performed to monitor dilatation once identified
  • Look for complications (perforation, pneumatosis)

Clinical:

  • Systemic toxicity (fever, tachycardia, high white count, etc) in the context of colitis.

As a radiologist, your job is to flag the colonic dilatation and look for complications.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 2 - Free intraperitoneal gas, perforated viscus

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Free intraperitoneal gas. There is gas seen under both right and left diaphragm on upright film. Gas rises, and so will collect here on upright films.
  • On supine films, notice the free gas is less apparent (but visible)

Other Findings:

  • Note of right common iliac stent
  • Moderate stool in the colon
  • Phleboliths in pelvis

Free intraperitoneal gas raises suspicion for perforated viscus (in the absence of known recent surgery / intervention). This patient had CT that confirmed perforated viscus (see learning material).

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
You need to be able to identify free gas on abdominal plain film. This case shows a small volume of free intraperitoneal gas on upright films. Some teaching points:

  • Air under the right diaphragm is easiest to spot. Normally, there shouldn't be gas between liver and diaphragm (unless there is interposed colon)
  • You can also see gas under the left diaphragm here. The stomach in the LUQ also contains gas, so need to differentiate gas in the stomach (contained by stomach wall) vs free gas (outside of stomach and follows contour of diaphragm. In this case, you can see gas above the gastric fundus (see arrows).

Basic Concept: Ddx for Gas Where it Shouldn't Be on Xray

  • Air from Outside Patient - i.e. recent surgery, tube placement, etc
  • Air from Inside Patient - i.e. perforated viscus (like in this case)
  • Air from Infection - i.e. gas forming organism (++ rare for peritoneal gas)


In this case, CT was performed that confirmed free gas and the cause.

Free Intraperitoneal Gas:


The Cause: Perforated Sigmoid Colon

﻿

Notice extraluminal locules of gas. This is more apparent when scrolling through the CT. We cover these concepts in the CT course.

cases.clinicalHistory: Abdo pain, prior CT showed 8 mm R proximal ureteric stone

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• 8 mm calcification projected to the right of the mid to lower sacrum in the pelvis. This likely represents the right ureteric stone now positioned in the distal right ureter.
  • There is a second tiny calcification immediately superior measuring 0.3 cm which may represent an additional tiny ureteric stone.
  • Probable tiny stone visible over the upper pole of the right kidney measuring 0.3 cm. Single arrows on this image demonstrate the location of the kidneys (soft tissue attenuation).

Proximal right ureteric stone described in history on CT is now positioned in the distal right ureter. There is likely a second smaller ureteric stone immediately adjacent on the superior aspect.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Renal stones are mostly calcified / radio-opaque, so can be seen at Xray. But, since there are so many superimposed structures (including stool) they can be challenging to see. First line imaging for renal colic is CT (or US for young patients). Given low sensitivity of Abdominal Xray, use is relatively limited.

Specifically, AXR can be used for follow-up of known radio-opaque stones. Once identified on CT or US, an AXR may be performed to see if visible on Xray -- and then followed with Xray. AXR is also performed for treatment planning (i.e. pre shockwave lithotripsy).

In all honesty though, low dose CT approaches the dose of an AXR -- and is much better overall.

Classic Exam Question: What Stones are Radiolucent?

Most stones are radio-opaque. Some are lucent/not seen at Xray (or even CT). Rather than memorize disparate lists, its helpful to understand the typical densities by major stone type:

  • Calcium-based - very high attenuation (>700 HU), radio-opaque
  • Struvite - intermediate (often > 400 HU), usually visible, often seen in infection
  • Uric Acid - lower HU (270-500HU) - classically 'radiolucent' on Xray but seen at CT
  • Indinavir (drug) stones - may be very low attenuation, lucent on Xray and may not even be seen at CT

So the answer is generally Uric Acid and Indinavir stones clasically lucent at Xray; and only indinavir at CT (uric acid seen at CT). There are other rarer stone types beyond the scope here.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• No free gas
  • ﻿Thumbprinting of distal transverse colon - indicating colonic thickening. Other areas of thumbprinting also present elsewhere

Other findings:

  • Cholecystectomy clips

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Thumbprinting refers to focal rounded indentations projecting into gas filled colonic lumen -- resembling thumbprints. The best example in this case is labelled on this image here. This just indicates colonic thickening / edema. Differential is broad -- colitis, edema from other causes, etc. Generally can correlate with history, and consider CT to confirm or look for complications.

We show this case to demonstrate the finding, but of course Xray isn't performed for this specifically (extremely insensitive for colonic thickening).

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Massively dilated loop of bowel (sigmoid colon) with apposed walls centrally, giving a 'bean' appearance (coffee bean sign); also see other images like this supine with the same findings
  • Apex of coffee bean points toward
  • Sigmoid colon starts low in the pelvis, and extends high in the abdomen, above the transverse colon
  • Notice that the more proximal colon is dilated

Follow-up CT also demonstrated the volvulus (see CT here). Notice beaking and mesenteric twisting of sigmoid in the LLQ with very dilated sigmoid (see coronal as well).

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Sigmoid volvulus is the most common colonic volvulus, and classically involves torsion of the sigmoid on its mesentery, causing a closed loop large bowel obstruction. It is high risk for ischemia and perforation, and so should not be missed.

Know the classic appearance, which is specific; but know that Xray is 65-80% sensitive for sigmoid volvulus -- in equivocal or uncertain cases, do a CT.

Xray Findings to Know

Knowing the names is less important than understanding the appearance.

  • Coffee bean sign (shown here), also called "Inverted U" shape
  • White stripe sign (3 white lines: lateral margins and opposed walls in the middle)
  • Absence of rectal gas -- supportive of true obstruction. Dilatation of upstream bowel is also often present, but sigmoid dilatation is out of proportion.

Management:

  • Endoscopic detorsion (sigmoidoscopy) is the mainstay when non-ischemic. Can treat and assess for ischemia at once, and then a rectal decompression tube is left for 1-3 days. This is 70-90% successful.
  • Surgery considered to if endoscopic management fails, if recurrent, in emergency, or after detorsion to reduce recurrence (elective sigmoid resection)

Next Level Of Knowledge:

While the 'classic' sigmoid volvulus involves twisting on its mesentery (mesenteroaxial), organoaxial volvulus can also occur -- involving twisting of the sigmoid along its long axis. See this European Radiology article led by Satheesh Krishna that I'm also on exploring CT signs, direction of twist, and more: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39060490/

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 9 - Dilated Cecum, CT confirmed cecal volvulus

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• NG tube is in place with tip and sidehole in the stomach
  • Very dilated loop of colon is partially imaged. Notice the haustra (outpouchings) and haustral folds, confirming dilated colon

Findings are abnormal and prompted a CT scan (here) that confirmed cecal vovlulus.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Cecal volvulus occurs when the cecum twists or folds abnormally, causing closed-loop obstruction that puts the patient at high risk of ischemia. Patients with a more mobile right colon are at higher risk. It is less common than sigmoid volvulus but important -- it is a surgical emergency (due to ischemia / perf risk).

Types of Cecal Volvulus:

  • Type 1 (axial, along its own axis) and type 2 (loop/inversion, flips further to LUQ) both involve true torsion of the cecum +/- terminal ileum; and together are 90% of cases. Type 2 involves the classic appearance of dilated cecum extending into the LUQ.
  • Type 3 (cecal bascule) involves no torsion, but instead the cecum 'folds' anteriorly and superiorly dilates without torsion.

This image helps conceptualize this.

Imaging:

  • Xray can suggest the diagnosis, but sensitivity is low (classic findings on Xray in <50% of cases!) Look for a markedly dilated cecum in an abnormal location (classically LUQ), distal colon decompressed downstream of obstruction; you may see appendix to confirm its cecum.
  • CT is preferred - so proceed to CT if suspected. CT is highly sensitive with moderate specificity. It will show mesenteric whirl, birds beak, severe cecal distension (> 10 cm) and in an abnormal location.

Notice that on our follow-up CT, you can see these findings: whirl (see coronal images centred on image 64), very dilated cecum in an abnormal location.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Significant dilatation of the transverse colon (measures 13 cm) and right colon.
  • There is some stool seen in the descending colon and rectum, though largely decompressed.
  • Significant dilatation of the small bowel
  • No free intraperitoneal air

Findings suggest large bowel obstruction at the level of the splenic flexure. Dilatation of the small bowel, suggesting incompetent ileocecal valve. CT confirmed obstruction due to splenic flexure malignancy.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Large Bowel Obstruction involves mechanical obstruction of the colon. Like all obstructions, look for bowel dilatation upstream and decompressed bowel distally:

  • Dilated colon > 6 cm (cecum > 9 cm). Haustra and peripheral location confirm its colon.
  • Decompressed bowel distal to obstruction
  • Small bowel dilatation associated suggests incompetent IC valve. This allows colon to decompress into small bowel. A competent IC valve actually puts the colon at higher risk for ischemia ('closed loop type')

Causes of colonic obstruction:

  • Malignancy (like this case seen at CT)
  • Diverticulitis / stricture
  • Volvulus (sigmoid > cecal > transverse colon)

Look for signs of ischemia, perforation. Get a CT!

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 21 - VP shunt tubing disconnection / discontinuity

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• VP shunt catheter in the abdomen, with two tips seen in the pelvis (arrows). This tube is disconnected, with no tubing extending toward the chest (Normally, tube is continuous from ventricle to peritoneal cavity)
  • Proximal tip of catheter is in the midline (likely in ventricular system, single arrow), and burr hole is visible
  • Lateral skull Xray shows tip of shunt, tubing extending to a radiolucent reservoir, and the valve just inferior to that. Normally, tubing should be attached to the valve that extends caudally / towards the chest and abdomen in the patient. Here there is no tubing (site of disconnection).

Findings are in keeping with VP shunt disconnection / discontinuity just inferior to the valve. Distal tubing is isolated in the peritoneal cavity.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
CSF shunts are placed for long-term CSF diversion to treat hydrocephalus. Fluid drains from the ventricular system to another space. This is most commonly to peritoneal cavity (VP shunt = ventriculoperitoneal shunt, first choice) but can be non-peritoneal if needed (ventriculopleural, ventriculoatrial, etc).

A "shunt series" are Xrays performed to evaluate the shunt tubing from top to bottom (often involves skull, neck, chest, and abdominal Xray). You should look for: kinks, fractures, disconnections, migration. Confirm the distal tip reaches the intended destination.

Generally, shunt series should be used selectively when there is a clinical reason to check. Routine post-op shunt series are discouraged.


As an aside, the valve appearance can vary but is often programmable/ adjustable -- see the two radiopaque dots on ours, but it can look more like a dial. If you care to learn more about this, check out this article, including page 4 that looks like the one in this case.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 12 - Lead pipe appearance of colon in UC

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Bowel wall thickening of an 11cm segment of the descending colon, lacking normal haustral markings ('lead pipe appearance').
  • No bowel dilatation.
  • No Free Gas

Bowel wall thickening of a featureless segment of the descending colon. The patient had a known history of Ulcerative Colitis.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
A “lead pipe” colon refers to a featureless, straight, ahaustral (no outpouchings) segment of colon on imaging, reflecting chronic structural change with loss of haustrations.

This is most commonly (and classically) seen with chronic ulcerative colitis due to long-standing inflammation causing muscularis mucosae hypertrophy, strictures, submucosal fat deposition, and extramural fat proliferation, which reduce distensibility and efface haustra. It is a classic chronic change, often most evident in the left colon, due to fibrosis rather than active inflammation. But of course can have acute on chronic inflammation.


While classically described in UC, a “featureless” or “lead pipe” appearance can be seen after other chronic insults (e.g., chronic GVHD, Crohn, radiation).

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Rigler's sign (lucency/gas on both sides of the bowel wall), indicating free gas on supine film (arrows)
  • Lucent liver in the right upper quadrant (circle)
  • Prior Aortoiliac Stent (prior EVAR)

Supine only films are much less sensitive for free gas. But, there are a few signs to be aware of. This patient had CT, confirming free gas and perforated viscus:

teachingSections.name: Learning Material:
teachingSections.html:
When a patient is lying supine (back down), gas will rise to the front of the abdomen and is superimposed over other structures. Thus, you won't generally see free gas 'under the diaphragm' like on upright films. And, supine films are thus much less sensitive for picking up free gas!

Even in this case, where there is a moderate volume of gas, the supine Xray findings are somewhat challenging, with high interobserver variability.

Still, you should know some of the most classic signs of free gas described on supine film:

  • Rigler's Sign - the poster child. Gas on both sides of bowel wall is dark, which accentuates / outlines the bowel wall itself. This is classic and one you should now. That being said, since bowel loops overlap one another, it can be easy to overcall as well in real life.
  • Falciform Ligament Sign - gas outlines the falciform in the RUQ
  • Football sign - massive volume collects anteriorly, with lucency shaped like a football
  • Other named signs exist

For practical, real life radiology:

If you need to determine if there is free gas on a plain film, get an upright film. If the patient can't sit upright, you can do a cross-table lateral or decubitus view (patient on side). Or, in real life, just get a CT!!! As introduced in the video, abdominal plain films have only a few real indications -- and relying on a supine film to detect free gas is not one of them...

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 7 - Small Bowel Obstruction with Fluid Filled Loops, String of Pearls Sign

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Very little air in the small bowel, except for a string of pearls sign in the proximal jejunum. This is an obliquely oriented row of small gas bubbles in fluid filled small bowel loops.
  • Fluid-filled loops of small bowel (window and notice 'soft tissue' attenuation tubular appearance of distended fluid filled loops)

Other findings:

  • No free air
  • There is new osteosclerotic lesion in the right fifth rib anteriorly.
  • Cholecystectomy clips and surgical clips in the pelvis

SUMMARY:

Findings are highly suspicious for small bowel obstruction.

CT was performed that confirmed the obstruction (see below). Notice that the dilated loops are fluid filled, and so don't show up as obviously on plain film.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material:
teachingSections.html: Abdominal Xray is not very sensitive for bowel obstruction. But, when specific features are present, we can be confident that there is likely an obstruction. We demonstrated the most classic in the prior case. One uncommon but important appearance to be aware of is shown here. When the dilated loops are fluid filled, we don't see them very well on Xray. But, trapped locules of gas within the small bowel on upright films can become trapped and give this 'string of pearls' appearance that is relatively specific for obstruction! This case is classic.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
Multiple foreign bodies:

  • Paperclip projecting over right colon / ascending colon (see lateral)
  • Tiny round radiopaque objects in the left abdomen (single arrow), best seen when zooming in (see image below, too). These are small round magnets, presumably in small bowel.
  • Aside: these other radioopaque structures are outside of the patient (ECG lead stickers, bra clasp/hook). Notice them on the patient's belly skin on the lateral.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
We introduced foreign body ingestion in the prior case. It is important to look out for risky ingested objects, including batteries, sharps, and magnets. This case shows an example of small round magnets.

The appearance of magnets depends on the shape of the magnets, but clasically:

  • String of identical round densities, often touching or stacked
  • 2+ small round objects tightly apposed (like in our case)
  • Clinical history important of course

Magnets are a dangerous ingestion when 2+ magnets are ingested. A single magnet can be observed. With 2+, they can attract one another across bowel wall and cause pressure necrosis, perforation, or fistula. Thus, should be removed if proximal enough, and at minimum followed if more distal.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Dilated loops of small bowel (also see here on supine)
  • On upright film there are multiple differential air fluid levels (air fluid levels at different heights), including within the same bowel loop.
  • No free gas

Findings are highly suspicious for small bowel obstruction.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Abdominal Plain Film is not very sensitive for small bowel obstruction. If you see dilated loops of small bowel alone, that is relatively nonspecific -- and could be seen with ileus or obstruction, best differentiated at CT. However, the pattern can give you clues.

More importantly for real life practice -- there are a few more specific signs that strongly suggest obstruction over ileus.

This case demonstrates one of the more specific signs: on upright films, multiple air-fluid levels at different heights (multiple differential air fluid levels). Most specific is having air fluid levels in the same small bowel loop that differ in height by > 2 cm.


Remember, you will only see air-fluid levels on upright films (or decubitus), but not supine films. On supine films, you will see the pattern of dilatation best.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 15 - Incidental Gallstones, Chilaiditi's sign (Colonic Interposition)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• There are calcified, lamellated gallstones projecting over the right upper quadrant.
  • Colonic interposition between right liver and right diaphragm (Chilaiditi's sign = colonic interposition mimicking free gas, notice the colonic features including haustral folds)

Other findings:

  • Arterial calcification. Surgical clips in the left inguinal region.
  • No findings of obstruction
  • Generalized osteopenia. Degenerative changes in the hips and spine.

No bowel obstruction or free gas. Gallstones noted.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
There are no acute findings on this Xray. But, we are showing the case to demonstrate two things: the appearance of calcified gallstones, and Chilaiditi's sign.

Colonic Interposition (Chilaiditi's sign) is important to know about because the appearance of gas under the right diaphragm can mimic appearance of free gas. In this case it is clearly colon, and you can usually tell by the appearance (separation between diaphragm and gas, morphology of bowel loop rather than following contour of diaphragm, and haustral folds/haustra). When in doubt, can perform a CT.

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Diffuse mixed sclerotic and lytic appearance of the bones, including spine, pelvic bones, and ribs (arrow to examples)
  • Bilateral femoral neck pathologic fractures (labelled)
  • Nonspecific bowel gas pattern with mild distension but no specific features of obstruction on supine film (not well evaluated).

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
This is a case of bilateral pathologic femoral neck fractures on the background of known myeloma. We will cover MSK plain film concepts in a separate course. We show this case as a reminder to look at all of the bones. This case is not subtle -- there is diffuse abnormality of the bones and bilateral femoral neck fractures. You may see isolated femoral fractures, lesions, etc that may be much more subtle, so be on the lookout!

In real life, the patient history and prior imaging will be visible to you. For patients who have malignancy with multiple follow-up CTs, the findings you make on abdominal Xray are most likely known. You should check and dictate accordingly (i.e. known vs new).

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Air within the bowel wall = Pneumatosis Intestinalis. This is most apparent in the cecum (RLQ, arrows), but also seen in multiple other loops of bowel on Xray
  • Bowel loops are also distended

Other Findings:

  • Rectal tube present
  • Partially imaged weighted tip feeding tube
  • Surgical clips and surgical staples

Pneumatosis Intestinalis should be considered bowel ischemia/infarction until proven otherwise. Urgent CT confirmed the finding of infarcted small and large bowel.


CT demonstrating pneumatosis in cecal loops (and small bowel loops):


CT correlate of distended small bowel loop with pneumatosis in LUQ:

teachingSections.name: Learning Material:
teachingSections.html:
Pneumatosis and portal venous gas are late findings of bowel ischemia / infarction that can be seen on Xray. Xray is not performed explicitly to look for bowel ischemia (far too insensitive), but you will see this on Xray occasionally and need to identify it.

Pneumatosis is gas within the bowel wall. It appears as lucency outlining the bowel wall rather than within the lumen. This tends to appear linear / curvilinear and parallelling the bowel contour.


Pseudo-pneumatosis is when gas gets trapped between stool and bowel wall, which can mimic pneumatosis. These are best differentiated on CT - covered in CT course. The presence of portal venous gas / gas in the mesentery confirms true pneumatosis.


As stated in the CT course, there are many causes of pneumatosis. But, unexplained pneumatosis should be considered bowel ischemia/infarction until proven otherwise. There is a laundry list of causes, but other practical ones to know include:

  • Anything that causes mucosal injury (injury at scope, infection, ulceration, etc)
  • Benign pneumatosis (chemotherapy, steroid use, etc) - occasionally you see patients that are very well with extensive pneumatosis on outpatient imaging.

Even in this latter group, patients should be evaluated to exclude any sinister cause of pneumatosis before assuming it is 'benign'.

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 16 - Incidental Porcelain Gallbladder, Moderate rectosigmoid stool

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Moderate volume of stool in the rectum and rectosigmoid. Within the remainder the colon, scattered gas and small amounts of stool.
  • No findings of obstruction.
  • No free gas.
  • Calcification in the right upper quadrant is in keeping with a porcelain gallbladder (calcified wall), confirmed at prior CT.

Other findings:

  • Pacemaker leads project over the heart, partially imaged
  • There are severe arthritic changes within the right hip with complete loss of joint space, subchondral sclerosis, deformity of the femoral head, and subchondral cyst formation along with the adjacent osteophytes

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
There are no acute findings on this Xray. We show this case to demonstrate the incidental porcelain gallbladder. Here, you can identify the calcification / morphology and postulate it may be a porcelain gallbladder, but on Xray it's impossible to know for sure (i.e. could be a calcified cyst or similar). A prior CT confirmed it in this case.

As mentioned in the introductory video, Abdominal Xray has overall limited utility. My simplified approach in the video highlights what you will see best: i.e. things that are too dark (gas -- including pattern in the bowel, gas where it shouldn't be), and things that are too bright (i.e. lines/tubes/iatrogenic, calcified things, etc). Here we show a calcified gallbladder wall in porcelain gallbladder.


Gallbladder wall calcification can occur due to chronic inflammation. This was classically described as a 'premalignant' condition. But, true cancer risk is relatively low (1-6%). Gallbladder is generally removed if patient is symptomatic or there is a higher risk pattern on imaging (incomplete calcification, or any suspicion for a mass).


As far as 'constipation' goes, my personal way of dictating these is just to describe the pattern and volume of stool in the colon. I don't report 'constipation' or 'fecal loading'. I just say "small/moderate/large volume stool in the (insert part(s) of colon)".

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• 3 round radiopaque foreign bodies projecting over the left mid abdomen
  • Zooming in, these have a double ring appearance due to a circular lucency (see image below) -- typical of button batteries.

  • At minimum, follow-up imaging suggested to ensure passage.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
Foreign body ingestion is not uncommon. There are a few items that are critical to recognize. While button bateries can mimic coins, they have a distinct appearance that is important to identify, shown in this case (double ring). That's the key takeaway for you as a radiologist.


Button batteries are dangerous and ingestion can be fatal. They can burn through tissue (d/t electric current causing very high pH / alkaline chemical burns) and cause ulceration, bleeding, or perforation. When seen in the esophagus, they should be removed endoscopically urgently. Even 'dead batteries' can injure tissue.

Once beyond the stomach, most will pass, but should be followed on serial radiographs.

Button batteries - explaining appearance on Xray:


Other important foreign bodies to identify:

  • Magnets - especially when multiple
  • Sharp pointed objects (needles, blades, etc)
  • Large objects that may not pass
  • Radiolucent items may not be seen (consider CT) - i.e. plastic, wood, thin glass, etc.

Always report location, number, size, progression on serial imaging, and look for complications!

cases.diagnosisTitle: Case 1 - NG tube in right mainstem bronchus

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• No NG tube in the stomach (expected location in the left upper quadrant)
  • NG tube in the airway, passing the right mainstem bronchus into right lower lobe airwa (arrow, labelled tip). The right mainstem bronchus and left mainstem bronchus (darker airways) are also labelled on the image.
  • The sidehole of the NG tube is also labelled (zoom in - notice the interruption in the radio-opaque marker where the arrow points)
  • Dilated loops of bowel partially imaged in the abdomen

The NG tube is malpositioned, with tip in the airway. This requires repositioning, and the team should be contacted urgently.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material
teachingSections.html:
NG Tube Placement is one of the more common reasons for an abdominal plain film that is still performed in practice. Your main task is to confirm that the tip and side hole are within the stomach.

Normal Position:

  • The tube should follow the course of the esophagus, pass below the diaphragm, with the tip and side hole in the stomach (left upper quadrant).
  • Ideally, the tip should be at least 10 cm below the diaphragm.

Malposition:

This case shows malpositioned tube, with tip in the right bronchus. Patients are at risk for lung injury (i.e. aspiration if used), pneumothorax, infection/emypema. The team should be contacted, and the tube should not be used.

Most common types of malposition:

  • Esophageal / too proximal (most common): tip in esophagus +/- curved, or side hole above GE junction (suggest advancement / repositioning)
  • Coiled in upper airway
  • Tracheobronchial placement (like our case in right mainstem)

PEARL: If the history is for NGT placement, and you don't see the NG tube -- look closely at the top of the film (like in this case), or ask to image higher up!

Types of Tube:

  • Single bore, non-weighted tip (Levin Tube) - like in this case. Side hole generally seen as interruption in radio-opaque marker
  • Weighted tip NG Tubes - these have distal metallic weights that are much more radio-opaque (whiter)

teachingSections.name: Key Findings
teachingSections.html:
• Surgical Staples from recent surgery
  • Left Lower Quadrant Retained Sponge, seen due to radioopaque marker as a serpiginous / linear band ('twisted ribbon') in the operative field

Other Findings:

  • Two JP drains (Jackson-Pratt Drains) present (double arrows)
  • NG Tube with tip in the stomach
  • Left inguinal hernia repair with mesh (single arrows to small radio-opaque tacks)
  • Lung findings were assessed on a separate chest Xray (covered in CXR course)

The finding of a retained sponge must be communicated directly with the surgical team.

teachingSections.name: Learning Material:
teachingSections.html:
In the OR, when the sponge/ instrument count is off, an Xray is performed to assess for any retained surgical material. Most of these are negative. But. they often call for a read on these while in the OR, so you need to know what to look for. We show this case to demonstrate what a surgical sponge looks like on Xray. Namely, the radio-opaque serpiginous marker.

In addition, there are a few other things we see on this Xray that you need to know:

  • Look for other retained materials: needles (curvilinear needle driver), foreceps/scissors, etc. Talk to the team to see how worried they are and what counts are off. Describe what you see (i.e. they may have purposely left something behind like a sponge).
  • JP Drains - these are surgical drains connected to tubing with negative pressure post-op -- used to evacuate fluid after surgery. They have a smooth tubular appearance like shown here (double arrows). You see these all the time post op.
  • At hernia repair with a mesh, you often see small metallic tacks to fix the mesh (single arrow, image below). Here we see that in the left inguinal region from a prior hernia repair. We don't see the mesh itself. Here is a video of tacks being placed at mesh repair.
